items.prompt: The indefinite integral of f represents:
items.answer: the entire class of antiderivatives F(x) + C

the area under f from a to b

items.prompt: Two indefinite integrals having the same derivative:
items.answer: differ only by a constant (same family of curves)

expected.correct: differ only by a constant (same family of curves)
options: differ only by a constant (same family of curves)
differ by a factor of x

items.prompt: To integrate an expression containing x^2 + 1 and a factor of x, a useful substitution is:
A rational function P(x)/Q(x) is called proper when:
items.answer: the degree of P(x) is less than the degree of Q(x)

expected.correct: the degree of P(x) is less than the degree of Q(x)
options: the degree of P(x) is less than the degree of Q(x)
the degree of P(x) is greater than the degree of Q(x)

items.prompt: Partial fraction decomposition is applied to a proper rational function in order to:
items.answer: write it as a sum of simpler fractions that are easy to integrate

expected.correct: write it as a sum of simpler fractions that are easy to integrate
options: write it as a sum of simpler fractions that are easy to integrate

items.prompt: Using integration by parts, the integral of x cos x dx equals:
items.answer: x sin x + cos x + C

expected.correct: x sin x + cos x + C
options: x sin x + cos x + C
x sin x - cos x + C
x cos x + sin x + C

items.prompt: By the first Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, if A(x) is the area function of a continuous f, then A'(x) equals:

Let f(x) = 2x and f⁻¹(y) = y/2. Using the inverse-function integral identity ∫_a^b f(x)dx + ∫_{f(a)}^{f(b)} f⁻¹(y)dy = b·f(b) − a·f(a) with a=0, b=3 (so f(0)=0, f(3)=6), compute the value of ∫_0^3 2x dx + ∫_0^6 (y/2) dy.
By the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, the definite integral of 2x dx from 0 to 3 (an antiderivative is x^2) equals:
State the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (Theorem 2) and outline how you use it to evaluate a definite integral of a continuous function f on [a, b].

expected.rubric: If f is continuous on [a, b] and F is an antiderivative of f, then the definite integral of f from a to b equals F(b) - F(a). To use it: find any one antiderivative F of f (no constant of integration needed), evaluate F at the upper limit b and the lower limit a, and subtract: F(b) - F(a).
markPoints: Integral of f from a to b = F(b) - F(a) for an antiderivative F
Find any antiderivative F of f (constant not needed)
Evaluate F(b) and F(a) and subtract
